sections.section_title: 3.1 Health: Is It More Than Not Falling Sick?

content.text: Health is much more than simply not having diseases. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), health is defined as 'a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease.' This definition highlights three key aspects: physical well-being (body functioning properly), mental well-being (positive mindset and emotional health), and social well-being (healthy relationships and social connections).

A truly healthy person can perform various tasks efficiently, cope well in different and difficult situations, and adjust well with peer groups and other members of society. Health includes feeling good physically, staying positive emotionally, and maintaining strong relationships with others.

A student who moved to a new school felt lonely due to no friends and busy parents. He spent excessive time on social media, which made him feel worse. He experienced headaches, lost weight, couldn't sleep well, and had other health problems. His physical symptoms were linked to his mental and social struggles. With counselling support and help making friends, his overall health improved.

Ayurveda, India's traditional medicine system, teaches that true health is a balance of body, mind, and surroundings. This ancient wisdom emphasizes maintaining this balance through daily and seasonal routines.

items: Dinacharya (daily routine): Following a consistent daily schedule maintains balanced health
Ritucharya (seasonal routine): Adapting habits to seasonal changes supports well-being
Prakriti-based diet: Eating fresh, wholesome food suited to one's body constitution (prakriti)
Lifestyle practices: Regular exercise, cleanliness, restful sleep, and a calm mind support overall well-being
Yoga and meditation: Practices like yoga, meditation, and mindfulness enhance physical and mental health

content.text: Staying healthy involves adopting good habits that support both physical and mental well-being. These habits should become part of daily routine for long-term health benefits.

items: Keep yourself clean and maintain personal hygiene—wash hands frequently, bathe regularly, maintain dental hygiene
Eat a healthy and balanced diet—include fruits, vegetables, whole grains; avoid processed, fatty, and sugary foods
Exercise regularly—engage in physical activities like playing outdoors, walking, running, cycling, or sports
Make time to relax or meditate every day—practice yoga or breathing exercises (pranayama)
Get enough sleep—allow your body and mind proper rest and recovery (7-8 hours for students)
Manage stress—practice relaxation techniques and positive thinking
Say no to harmful substances—avoid tobacco, alcohol, and addictive drugs

content.text: Certain habits negatively impact health and should be avoided to maintain well-being.

items: Spending excessive time on mobile phones or digital screens—reduces physical activity and affects eyes, posture, and sleep
Eating fast food and junk food daily—lacks nutrients and contributes to obesity and related diseases
Sleeping very late or getting insufficient sleep—impairs physical recovery and mental function
Skipping meals, especially breakfast—leads to nutritional deficiencies and low energy
Smoking or using tobacco—causes serious health complications
Excessive screen time before bed—disrupts sleep quality

content.text: Beyond personal habits, the environment we live in significantly affects health. Clean surroundings reduce disease transmission and improve overall well-being.

items: Clean air and water—essential for proper breathing and hydration; air pollution causes coughing and asthma
Sanitary living conditions—reduce breeding sites for disease-carrying insects and pathogens
Air Quality Index (AQI)—helps assess air cleanliness; important indicator in cities with pollution
Clean surroundings—well-maintained areas reduce insect breeding and disease transmission

content.text: Mental health is equally important as physical health. Even with good eating and clean living, loneliness or emotional distress affects overall health. Spending time with friends and family, talking, laughing, and having fun are essential for mental well-being.

sections.section_title: 3.3 How Do We Know That We Are Unwell?

content.text: When our body is not functioning properly, we experience changes that indicate illness. Understanding the difference between symptoms and signs helps in communication with healthcare providers.

items: Symptoms: What we feel internally but cannot be measured or seen by others (pain, tiredness, dizziness, nausea, difficulty breathing)
Signs: Observable or measurable changes detected by healthcare providers or instruments (fever, rash, swelling, high blood pressure, bruising)

content.text: When multiple symptoms and signs appear together, they indicate a specific disease or condition. A fever with sore throat and cough suggests a cold or flu. A rash with itching and blisters suggests chickenpox. These manifestations help doctors diagnose and treat the underlying condition.

sections.section_title: 3.4 Diseases: What Are the Causes and Types?

content.text: A disease is a condition that affects the normal working of the body or mind. It occurs when one or more organs or organ systems stop functioning properly. Diseases are classified into two major types based on their causes and transmission.
Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens and can spread from one person to another. Examples: typhoid, dengue, flu, chickenpox, COVID-19, tuberculosis, measles.

Non-communicable diseases are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from person to person. They are usually linked to lifestyle, diet, genetics, and/or environment. Examples: cancer, diabetes, asthma, heart disease, obesity, high blood pressure. These have become the leading cause of death in India.
Pathogens are disease-causing organisms that enter the body and cause infection. Types of pathogens include:

items: Bacteria—single-celled organisms; cause typhoid, cholera, tuberculosis
Viruses—smaller than bacteria; cause common cold, flu, chickenpox, measles, COVID-19
Fungi—organisms like molds and yeasts; cause skin infections
Worms (Helminths)—parasitic organisms; cause ascariasis and other intestinal infections
Protozoa—single-celled organisms; cause malaria, dysentery

content.text: Communicable diseases spread from infected persons to healthy persons through various routes of transmission.

items: Airborne transmission: Through coughing, sneezing, or respiratory droplets (common cold, flu, chickenpox, measles, tuberculosis)
Direct contact: Touching infected person or contaminated surfaces (chickenpox, impetigo)
Indirect contact: Sharing personal items like towels, utensils, or clothing of infected person
Contaminated food and water: Through consumption of food or water contaminated with pathogens (typhoid, cholera, hepatitis A)
Vector-borne transmission: Through insects that carry pathogens, especially mosquitoes (malaria, dengue) and houseflies (diarrheal diseases)

content.text: Some communicable diseases are caused by parasitic worms that live inside the body, especially in the digestive system. These worms feed on nutrients and live as parasites. They spread through contaminated food, water, soil, or contact with infected people.
Understanding common communicable diseases, their transmission, and prevention methods helps protect ourselves and others.

rows: Nasal congestion, sore throat, fever, cough, body ache
Hand washing, avoid sharing items, cover mouth/nose, vaccination
Mild fever, itchy skin, rashes, blisters
Fever, sore throat, reddish rashes on skin
Cough, fever, fatigue, night sweats, loss of appetite
Avoid close contact with TB patients, vaccination, good hygiene
Fatigue, fever, nausea, vomiting, jaundice, abdominal pain
Boiled water, proper food handling, vaccination
Good sanitation, boiled water, proper food, vaccination
Worms in stool, loss of appetite, poor growth, diarrhoea, weight loss, anaemia
Good hygiene, boiled water, proper food handling, sanitation
High fever, profuse sweating, periodic chills, body ache
Mosquito nets, repellents, long-sleeved clothes, mosquito control
High fever, headache, muscle and joint pain, nausea
Mosquito nets, repellents, avoid standing water, mosquito control

content.text: Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) are chronic conditions not caused by pathogens. They develop over time due to lifestyle, genetic, and environmental factors and have become the leading cause of death in India.

items: Diabetes: Caused by hormonal imbalances, unhealthy diet, lack of exercise, obesity; India has high prevalence
Heart disease: Linked to unhealthy diet, stress, smoking, lack of exercise, obesity
Cancer: Related to tobacco use, diet, pollution, genetic factors, and unhealthy lifestyle
Asthma: Triggered by allergens, air pollution, genetic predisposition, environmental factors
High blood pressure: Caused by salt intake, stress, obesity, lack of exercise
Obesity: Results from excessive calorie intake and insufficient physical activity
Deficiency diseases: Caused by lack of specific nutrients (scurvy-vitamin C deficiency, anaemia-iron deficiency, goitre-iodine deficiency)

content.text: Chronic diseases like cancer, diabetes, and asthma persist for more than 3 months and require long-term management. Prevention through healthy lifestyle changes is more effective than treatment after the disease develops.

sections.section_title: 3.5 How to Prevent and Control Diseases?

content.text: The phrase 'Prevention is better than cure' emphasizes that protecting ourselves from diseases is more effective than treating them after they occur. Simple hygiene and sanitation measures significantly reduce disease spread.

items: Keep yourself and surroundings clean—reduces pathogen breeding and transmission
Practice basic personal hygiene daily—washing hands, bathing, maintaining cleanliness
Wash hands frequently with soap and water—removes pathogens effectively
Cover mouth and nose while coughing or sneezing—prevents airborne disease transmission
Wear masks in crowded places—provides protection against airborne pathogens
Stay home when unwell—allows body recovery and prevents disease spread to others
Maintain clean drinking water and food—proper sanitation and water treatment prevent waterborne diseases

Community sanitation initiatives—improving toilet access and reducing open defecation significantly reduces disease spread (example: Odisha sanitation campaign reduced diarrhoea and childhood infections)

content.text: The immune system is the body's natural defense mechanism that fights against diseases. Some people get sick more frequently than others because of differences in their immune response—the natural ability of the body to fight diseases is called immunity.
Two types of immunity exist: (1) Natural immunity: Inborn resistance to certain diseases. (2) Acquired immunity: Protection developed after exposure to a pathogen or vaccine.

Vaccines are one of the most effective public health interventions. They train the immune system to recognize and attack harmful germs, providing protection against serious diseases without causing the disease itself.

How vaccines work: Vaccines contain weakened or dead pathogens, inactive harmful parts of pathogens, or genetic instructions that cause the body to make harmless pathogen parts. The immune system learns to recognize these components and develops antibodies. If the real pathogen invades later, the immune system quickly recognizes and eliminates it.

items: Types of vaccines: Made from weakened pathogens, dead pathogens, inactive pathogen parts, or genetic instructions (newer vaccines)
Examples: Tetanus shot, polio vaccine, measles vaccine, hepatitis vaccine, COVID-19 vaccines
Benefits: Prevent serious illnesses, reduce disease spread, save millions of lives yearly, protect vulnerable populations

content.text: Important: Vaccines are preventive, not curative. They help prevent diseases before they happen but do not treat infections once someone is already sick. Vaccines are carefully tested for safety by scientists and doctors.

Historical example - Smallpox eradication: In the late 1700s, English doctor Edward Jenner discovered that people infected with cowpox (a milder disease) did not get smallpox. He tested cowpox material on a boy, who later showed no illness when exposed to smallpox. This led to the first vaccine and eventually the worldwide eradication of smallpox. In India, a traditional method called variolation was used, where material from smallpox sores was scratched onto skin to build immunity.

India's role in vaccine production: India is one of the world's largest vaccine producers, manufacturing vaccines on massive scale and supplying to many countries. Indian vaccine companies played a key role during COVID-19 pandemic and continue supporting global health efforts.

When the immune system cannot protect against infection, doctors prescribe antibiotics. Antibiotics are medicines that kill bacteria causing bacterial infections. They do not work against viral infections or diseases caused by protozoa—antibiotics specifically target bacterial cells.

Discovery of penicillin: In 1928, Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin, the first antibiotic. He noticed that a mold on a discarded petri dish prevented bacteria growth. This chance observation led to discovering that the mold released a substance killing bacteria, revolutionizing treatment of bacterial infections.

Antibiotic resistance: Despite their effectiveness, indiscriminate use of antibiotics has led to antibiotic resistance. Bacteria exposed to antibiotics that survive and multiply despite treatment, eventually becoming resistant to that antibiotic. This makes common infections harder to treat and increases complications, prolonged illness, and even death risk.

items: How resistance develops: Repeated or improper antibiotic use allows bacteria to adapt and develop resistance
Preventing resistance: Use antibiotics only when prescribed by doctors, in correct doses, for appropriate duration
Importance: Avoiding unnecessary use keeps antibiotics effective for future generations

content.text: Treatment approaches vary depending on disease type. For communicable diseases, antibiotics work against bacterial infections. For viral infections, supportive care helps the immune system fight the virus. For non-communicable diseases, management focuses on symptom control and improving quality of life.

Traditional medicine systems: Ayurveda, Siddha, and Unani have been used in India for centuries. They use natural substances like herbs, oils, and minerals for managing health problems. While useful for everyday well-being and some conditions, they may not be effective for all diseases. Scientific validation is important before relying on traditional treatments.

Management of non-communicable diseases: Treatment includes medication, lifestyle changes, and rehabilitation. Early diagnosis and continuous care are essential to control disease progression and prevent complications.

glossary.definition: A state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being—not merely the absence of disease. Includes feeling good physically, maintaining positive mindset, and having strong relationships.
A condition that affects the normal working of the body or mind; occurs when one or more organs or organ systems stop functioning properly.
What a person feels internally and experiences but cannot be directly seen or measured by others (e.g., pain, tiredness, dizziness, nausea).
An observable or measurable change detected by healthcare providers or instruments (e.g., fever, rash, high blood pressure, swelling).
A disease caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms) that can spread from one person to another through various routes of transmission.
A disease not caused by pathogens that does not spread from person to person; usually linked to lifestyle, diet, genetics, and/or environment.
A disease-causing organism including bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms, and protozoa that invades the body and causes infection.
The natural ability of the body to fight diseases; the resistance to pathogens developed through exposure or vaccination.
A biological preparation that trains the immune system to recognize and fight specific pathogens using weakened, dead, or harmless parts of the pathogen.
A medicine that kills bacteria and is used to treat bacterial infections; does not work against viral infections or protozoan diseases.
A phenomenon where bacteria survive and multiply despite treatment with antibiotics that previously killed them, making infections harder to treat.
An organism (usually an insect) that carries and transmits disease-causing pathogens from one host to another (e.g., mosquitoes transmit malaria and dengue).
Organisms that live in or on another living being and feed on its nutrients, often referring to parasitic worms in the digestive system.
The body's natural defense system that fights against harmful pathogens and protects against diseases.
A disease that persists for a long time (more than 3 months) and requires ongoing management and treatment.
Provision of clean and hygienic living conditions including clean water, proper waste disposal, and control of disease vectors.
A measurement system that indicates how clean or polluted the air is; important for assessing respiratory health risks.
Protection against disease developed after exposure to a pathogen or through vaccination; immune response learned by the body.
A non-communicable disease caused by lack of specific nutrients in diet (e.g., scurvy from vitamin C deficiency, anaemia from iron deficiency).
Breathing exercises practiced in yoga that promote mental and physical relaxation and well-being.

summary_points.description: Health means complete physical, mental, and social well-being—not just absence of disease. A healthy person can perform tasks efficiently, cope with difficulties, and maintain good relationships. Health depends on lifestyle choices, environmental factors, and emotional well-being.

Staying healthy requires eating balanced nutritious diet, regular exercise, adequate sleep, good hygiene, stress management, and avoiding harmful substances. These habits support both physical and mental health. Equally important are clean environment, access to pure water and clean air.
Symptoms (what we feel internally) and signs (observable changes) indicate illness. Understanding these helps communication with doctors for accurate diagnosis and treatment of underlying conditions.

Communicable diseases spread from person to person through pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms) via airborne, contact, food/water, or insect transmission. Non-communicable diseases result from lifestyle, genetics, and environment and cannot spread between people. India's leading cause of death has shifted from communicable to non-communicable diseases.

Simple measures prevent communicable disease spread: maintain hygiene, wash hands frequently, cover mouth/nose when coughing, avoid sharing personal items, drink boiled water, keep surroundings clean. Staying home when unwell and community sanitation initiatives significantly reduce disease transmission.

Non-communicable diseases like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer develop due to unhealthy lifestyle. Prevention includes balanced diet, regular exercise, stress management, avoiding tobacco/alcohol, and maintaining healthy weight. Deficiency diseases result from nutritional lack and can be prevented through proper diet.

The immune system is the body's natural defense against pathogens. Vaccines train the immune system using weakened or harmless pathogen parts, providing acquired immunity without causing disease. Vaccines are preventive (prevent disease before it happens) and not curative. Smallpox eradication demonstrates vaccine effectiveness.

Antibiotics kill bacteria causing bacterial infections (discovered 1928) but do not work against viral or protozoan infections. Indiscriminate use leads to antibiotic resistance where bacteria survive treatment. Use antibiotics only when prescribed by doctors in correct doses for appropriate duration to preserve their effectiveness.

Communicable disease treatment depends on pathogen type—antibiotics for bacteria, supportive care for viruses. Non-communicable disease management focuses on symptom control, lifestyle changes, and prevention of complications through early diagnosis and continuous care. Traditional medicine systems can complement conventional treatment for overall well-being.
